title: Sew a simple drawstring bag
subtitle: Two rectangles, one casing, one cord. The starter sewing project that teaches three machine techniques in one bag.
excerpt: Two rectangles of cotton stitched on three sides, a single casing folded along the top, and a cord drawn through. Sized for a shoe bag, a swimming bag, or a fabric gift wrap. 25 cm wide by 35 cm tall when finished. Machine or hand sewn.
sourceNotes:
Construction drawn from two public-domain references:

• Beeton's Book of Needlework (1870), §Plain Bags. Project Gutenberg #25640. The single-channel casing construction is the standard Victorian make-do bag.
• Make Do and Mend (UK Board of Trade, 1943), reissued as a public-domain wartime pamphlet. The shoe bag from a flour sack uses the same pattern.

recipeTools.notes: fabric shears if you have them; craft scissors otherwise.

glossaryTerms.definition: A folded-and-stitched channel sewn along the top of a bag for the drawstring to thread through. The casing is wider than the cord so the cord can move freely.
The factory-finished edges of a piece of woven fabric that run parallel to the lengthwise grain. The selvedge doesn't fray and is sometimes used as the cut edge of a casing to save hemming.
Placing two pieces of fabric with their patterned, finished sides facing each other before sewing. The seam is hidden inside when the piece is turned right-side out.

content.text: A drawstring bag is the simplest possible finished sewing project. Two rectangles of cotton fabric, one machine seam down each side and across the bottom, one folded casing along the top, and a cord drawn through. The finished bag is 25 cm wide by 35 cm tall: about right for a pair of plimsolls, a swimming costume and towel, or a gift bag in place of paper wrapping. The whole project takes an evening on a sewing machine or two evenings by hand.

What it teaches: a basic seam, a folded hem, and a casing for a drawstring. These are three of the six or seven techniques that show up in every sewn bag, garment, and home-furnishing project from here on. A drawstring bag with neat seams is a real piece of household kit; once made, the same pattern scales up to a laundry bag and down to a small jewellery pouch.
What you need

Half a metre of medium-weight cotton fabric. Cotton canvas, cotton drill, calico, or a sturdy quilting cotton all work; avoid anything stretchy or slippery for the first attempt. A polyester thread to match the fabric (or cotton thread if the bag will be washed at high heat). About 1.5 metres of cotton drawstring cord or strong tape (a shoelace works in a pinch).
Fabric scissors, a tape measure, an iron, pins, a sewing machine if you have one (or a hand-sewing needle and the
backstitch
for the whole bag). A safety pin to feed the cord through the casing at the end.
Cutting the rectangles

Cut two rectangles of fabric, each 28 cm wide by 42 cm tall. That allows for a 1.5 cm seam allowance down each side, a 1.5 cm seam allowance across the bottom, and a 4 cm folded casing at the top. Mark a 4 cm fold line in pencil along the top edge of each piece (use the back of the fabric, on the wrong side).
If the fabric has
selvedges
(factory-finished edges), arrange the cut so each rectangle's side edges run parallel to the selvedge. That keeps the cotton from skewing as it's stitched.
Sewing the three sides
Lay the two rectangles
right sides together
. The finished sides face in; the wrong sides face out. Pin all the way down each side and across the bottom (skip the top edge for now).
Stitch down one side at 1.5 cm from the edge, stopping 5 cm short of the top (this leaves a gap for the casing to open at the side seam). Backstitch over the start and end of the stitch line.
Repeat down the other side, again stopping 5 cm short of the top.
Stitch across the bottom at 1.5 cm from the edge. Backstitch at both corners.
Trim the corners diagonally (don't cut into the stitching) so they sit flat when the bag turns right-side out. Press the side seams open with the iron.
Folding the casing
Turn the top raw edge under by 1 cm (towards the wrong side) all the way round, and press.
Fold the top down again by 3 cm (along the pencil line marked earlier), and press. The folded casing now sits along the inside of the bag.
Pin around the casing, then stitch along the lower edge of the fold (closest to the body of the bag) all the way round. Catch the side seams cleanly; don't sew over the 5 cm gaps left earlier.
The
casing
now runs all the way round the top of the bag, with two openings at the side seams.
Threading the cord

Cut the drawstring cord in two equal lengths (each about 70 cm). Pin one end of the first cord to a safety pin. Feed the safety pin through one of the side openings, all the way round inside the casing, and out the same side opening. Knot the two ends of the cord together loosely.

Take the second cord; pin it to the safety pin. Feed it through the opposite side opening, all the way round the casing the other direction, and out the same side. Knot the ends loosely. The bag now has two cords, each entering and leaving through one side seam, with knotted ends sitting on the outside. Pulling one cord tightens the bag; pulling both together centres the gather and closes the top.
Common mistakes

attrs.heading: What goes wrong on a first drawstring bag
attrs.intro: Four common slips on a first project.

items.symptom: The casing is sewn shut at both sides, with no way to thread the cord
items.cause: The 5 cm gap at the top of each side seam was forgotten; the side seams were sewn all the way up.
items.fix: Use a seam ripper to open the side seams from the top edge down to 5 cm below the casing's bottom edge. The bag is still usable; just remember the gap next time.

items.symptom: The bag won't sit flat at the bottom; one corner sticks out
items.cause: The corner wasn't trimmed diagonally, so the folded fabric is bulkier than the rest of the seam.
items.fix: Turn the bag inside out, trim the corner diagonally without cutting the stitching, and turn back. Press the bottom flat with the iron.

items.symptom: Cord won't feed through the casing
items.cause: The casing is too narrow for the cord, or the stitching has caught a fold inside.
items.fix: Check the cord against the casing's width; a 3 cm casing fits cord up to about 1 cm wide. If the stitching caught a fold, unpick a short section, smooth the inside of the casing, and re-stitch.

items.symptom: Bag is wonky: one side longer than the other
items.cause: Cutting wasn't square (the two rectangles weren't identical) or the seam allowance varied.
items.fix: Measure and recut from a fresh piece of fabric; mark every line in pencil before cutting. Use the same seam allowance throughout (1.5 cm everywhere).

content.text: Where this goes next

Once a single drawstring bag is in hand, the same construction scales to a 50 by 70 cm laundry bag or down to a 10 by 15 cm jewellery pouch. The next sewing project that uses the same techniques is a tote bag with handles (rectangles plus the addition of strap tabs), then a simple cushion cover (rectangles plus an envelope back). Each adds one technique to the foundation set without abandoning what's already learned.
